name: Pediatrics: Childhood Communicable Diseases, Bioterrorism, Natural Disasters, and the Maternal-Child Patient

scenarios.question: Which statement best describes Health care?
options: This is not a recognized term in pediatric nursing
associated infection—an infection acquired after admission to a health care faci...
This is an outdated concept no longer taught
This only applies to adult patients, not children
scenarios.rationale: Health care: associated infection—an infection acquired after admission to a health care facility4 (Slide 4)

scenarios.question: When caring for pediatric patients, the nurse should:
options: Use the same approach as with adult patients
include Hand hygiene Necessity of immunizations Proper food storage Us
Exclude family members from care decisions
Focus only on the child's physical symptoms
scenarios.rationale: include Hand hygiene Necessity of immunizations Proper food storage Us. Family-centered, age-appropriate care is essential in pediatrics. (Slide 15)

scenarios.question: When caring for pediatric patients, the nurse should:
options: be available in unit where immunizations are given
Focus only on the child's physical symptoms
Exclude family members from care decisions
Use the same approach as with adult patients
scenarios.rationale: be available in unit where immunizations are given. Family-centered, age-appropriate care is essential in pediatrics. (Slide 19)

scenarios.question: Which of the following are expected findings in Childhood Communicable Diseases, Bioterrorism, Natural Disasters, and the Maternal-Child Patient? (Select all that apply.)
options: Prodromal period—time between earliest symptom and appearanc
Findings opposite to what was described
scenarios.rationale: Prodromal period—time between earliest symptom and appearanc is a characteristic finding. Review of Terms (1 of 2) Communicable disease—can be transmitted from one person... (Slide 3)

scenarios.question: Which of the following are expected findings in Childhood Communicable Diseases, Bioterrorism, Natural Disasters, and the Maternal-Child Patient? (Select all that apply.)
options: Hand hygiene Necessity of immunizations Proper food storage
Findings opposite to what was described
scenarios.rationale: Hand hygiene Necessity of immunizations Proper food storage  is a characteristic finding. Family Education to Prevent the Spread of Infection 15 Should include Hand hygie... (Slide 15)

scenarios.question: What is the primary treatment approach for this pediatric condition?
options: the following vaccines if patient is allergic to Baker’s yeast: Avoid
scenarios.rationale: the following vaccines if patient is allergic to Baker’s yeast: Avoid . Allergies and Toxicities 19 Epinephrine should be available in unit where immuni... (Slide 19)

scenarios.question: What is the incubation period for measles?
scenarios.rationale: Measles (rubeola): incubation 7-21 days (average 10-14). Prodrome: fever, cough, coryza, conjunctivitis. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: The characteristic rash of measles starts:
options: On the face and spreads downward
On the trunk and spreads outward
scenarios.rationale: Measles: maculopapular rash starts on face/hairline → downward over 3 days. Koplik spots in mouth. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Chickenpox is caused by:
scenarios.rationale: Varicella: highly contagious; crops of vesicles → crusts. Infectious 1-2 days before rash until all lesions crusted. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: A child with chickenpox should be isolated until:
scenarios.rationale: Varicella isolation: until all lesions are crusted (usually 5-7 days after rash onset). (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: What is the classic sign of roseola?
options: High fever for 3-5 days followed by rash
scenarios.rationale: Roseola (HHV-6): high fever 3-5 days, then defervescense → maculopapular rash on trunk. Common in infants. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Fifth disease (erythema infectiosum) is caused by:
scenarios.rationale: Fifth disease: slapped-cheek rash, lacy body rash. Parvovirus B19. Contagious before rash; no isolation needed once rash appears. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: A child with mumps presents with:
scenarios.rationale: Mumps: parotitis (unilateral or bilateral), fever, malaise. Orchitis in postpubertal males. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: What is the MMR vaccine schedule?
scenarios.rationale: MMR: first dose 12-15 months, second 4-6 years. Measles, Mumps, Rubella. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: What is the most concerning complication of mumps in adolescent males?
scenarios.rationale: Mumps orchitis: testicular inflammation; may cause atrophy. Sterility is rare but concerning. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Hand-foot-and-mouth disease is caused by:
scenarios.rationale: HFMD: vesicles on hands, feet, mouth (herpangina). Coxsackievirus. Supportive care. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Measles (rubeola) is prevented by:
options: MMR vaccine at 12-15 months and 4-6 years
scenarios.rationale: Measles: MMR vaccine. Highly contagious. Koplik spots (white spots in mouth) are pathognomonic. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Rubella (German measles) is concerning in:
scenarios.rationale: Rubella in pregnancy: teratogenic → deafness, cataracts, heart defects. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Pertussis (whooping cough) presents with:
options: Paroxysmal cough with whoop, post-tussive vomiting
scenarios.rationale: Pertussis: paroxysmal cough, inspiratory whoop, post-tussive emesis. Macrolide antibiotics. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Scarlet fever is caused by:
scenarios.rationale: Scarlet fever: strep + erythrogenic toxin → sandpaper rash, strawberry tongue, Pastia's lines. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Scarlet fever rash is described as:
scenarios.rationale: Scarlet fever: sandpaper rash, starts on trunk/neck, spares face, Pastia's lines (accentuated in creases). (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Varicella vaccine is given at:
scenarios.rationale: Varicella (chickenpox): 2 doses — 12-15 months and 4-6 years. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: A child with pertussis should be isolated for:
scenarios.rationale: Pertussis: isolate 5 days after antibiotics started (erythromycin/azithromycin). (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Rubeola (measles) prodrome includes:
scenarios.rationale: Measles prodrome: 3 C's (cough, coryza, conjunctivitis) + Koplik spots. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Mumps complications include:
scenarios.rationale: Mumps: parotitis, orchitis (postpubertal males), aseptic meningitis, pancreatitis. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Match each developmental theorist with their key concept.
scenarios.rationale: Erikson: psychosocial crises. Piaget: cognitive development. Kohlberg: moral reasoning. (Slide 15-20)

scenarios.question: Complete the statement about pediatric care.
scenarios.cloze_text: Pediatric nursing requires [[BLANK1]] care and consideration of [[BLANK2]] in all interactions.
scenarios.rationale: Family-centered, age-appropriate care is fundamental in pediatrics. (Slide 10)

scenarios.question: Click on the normal developmental findings.
scenarios.highlight_text: The child demonstrates age-appropriate milestones. Growth follows expected percentiles. The child engages with caregivers appropriately.
scenarios.rationale: All are normal pediatric findings. (Slide 25)

scenarios.question: A child presents with concerning developmental findings.
scenarios.rationale: Early identification and family support are key. (Slide 30)
